This table shows the activity of players of some game. Each row is a record of a player who logged in and played a number of games (possibly 0) before logging out on some day using some device. Write an SQL query that reports for each player and date, how many games played so far by the player. That is, the total number of games played by the player until that date. Check the example for clarity. The query result format is in the following example: Activity table: +----------- +----------- +------------ +-------------- + player_id device_id event_date games_played +----------- +----------- +------------ +-------------- + 1 2 2016-03-01 5 1 2 2016-05-02 6 1 3 2017-06-25 1 3 1 2016-03-02 0 3 4 2018-07-03 5 +----------- +----------- +------------ +-------------- + Result table: +----------- +------------ +--------------------- + player_id event_date games_played_so_far +----------- +------------ +--------------------- + 1 2016-03-01 5 1 2016-05-02 11 1 2017-06-25 12 3 2016-03-02 0 3 2018-07-03 5 +----------- +------------ +--------------------- + For the player with id 1, 5 + 6 = 11 games played by 2016-05-02, and 5 + 6 + 1 = 12 games played by 2017-06-25. For the player with id 3, 0 + 5 = 5 games played by 2018-07-03. Note that for each player we only care about the days when the player logged in. Solution 01/21/2020 (MySQL): select player_id, event_date, games_played_so_far from ( select player_id, event_date, if(@player = (@player := player_id) and @mydate < (@mydate := event_date), @games := @games + games_played, (@games := games_played)) as games_played_so_far, The Employee table holds all employees including their managers. Thomas Jefferson(id 6) is the last person to fit in the elevator because he has the last turn in these three people. These are some of the most commonly used string functions in SQL: ● UPPER(): Converts the value of a field to uppercase. ○ Example: SELECT UPPER('hello') → Result: 'HELLO' ● LOWER(): Converts the value of a field to lowercase. ○ Example: SELECT LOWER('HELLO') → Result: 'hello' ● LENGTH(): Returns the length of a text string. ○ Example: SELECT LENGTH('Database') → Result: 8 ● SUBSTRING(): Extracts a portion of a string. ○ Example: SELECT SUBSTRING('Database', 1, 4) → Result: 'Data' ● NOW(): Returns the current system date and time. ○ Example: SELECT NOW() → Result: 2024-09-06 12:34:56 ● FORMA T(): Formats a string according to specified formatting rules. ○ Example: SELECT FORMA T(1234.5678, 2) → Result: '1,234.57' ● CONCA T(): Concatenates two or more strings. ○ Example: SELECT CONCA T('SQL', ' Functions') → Result: 'SQL Functions' ● REPLACE(): Replaces all occurrences of a substring with a new substring. ○ Example: SELECT REPLACE('SQL Manual', 'Manual', 'Guide') → Result: 'SQL Guide' ● TRIM(): Removes leading and trailing spaces (or other specified characters) from a string. ○ Example: SELECT TRIM(' SQL ') → Result: 'SQL' Aggregate Functions: Aggregate functions are used to perform calculations on a set of values and return a single result. ● COUNT(): Returns the number of rows that match the query condition. ○ Example: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM students WHERE age > 18; ● SUM(): Adds the values of a specified column. ○ Example: SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees; ● AVG(): Returns the average value of a column. ○ Example: SELECT AVG(marks) FROM exams; ● MIN(): Returns the minimum value in a column. ○ Example: SELECT MIN(price) FROM products; ● MAX(): Returns the maximum value in a column. ○ Example: SELECT MAX(age) FROM users; ● ROUND(): Rounds a number to the specified number of decimal places. ○ Example: SELECT ROUND(123.456, 2) → Result: 123.46 SQL Operators

title: 266. ● dense_rank() : assigns a rank without gaps
description: Write a query to find the maximum salary within each department and list it alongside each instructor's details. Solution: SELECTname,dept_name,salary,MAX(salar y)OVER(PARTITIONBYdept_name)ASmax_salaryFROMinstructor; Section 2: Ranking Window Functions Ranking window functions are used to assign ranks or row numbers to rows in a dataset. Common functions include: ● RANK() : Assigns a rank to each row with ties, causing gaps in ranking. ● DENSE_RANK() : Assigns a rank without gaps. ● ROW_NUMBER() : Assigns a unique row number to each row. Example 2: RANK() Function for Instructors by Salary SELECTname,dept_name,salary,RANK()OVER(PARTITIONBYdept_nameORDERBYsalaryDESC)ASrank FROMinstructor; Explanation: ● This query ranks instructors within each department based on salary (highest salary gets rank 1). ● Tied salaries will receive the same rank, leaving gaps in the ranking. Practice Questions: Ranking Window Functions

title: 268. ● regexp : operator for regular expression pattern matching
description: Write a query using ROW_NUMBER() to assign unique row numbers to students in each department, ordered by their marks. Solution: SELECTname,dept_name,marks,ROW_NUMBER()OVER(PARTITIONBYdept_nameORDERBYmarksDESC)ASrow_numFROMstudent; Comparison: RANK() vs DENSE_RANK() vs ROW_NUMBER() Introduction to Regular Expressions in MySQL MySQL provides pattern matching operations through REGEXP (and its synonym RLIKE ) to enhance search utilities. Regular expressions help match text data based on complex patterns, offering more precision than basic search methods. ● REGEXP : Operator for regular expression pattern matching. ● RLIKE : Synonym for REGEXP. ● Case-Insensitive : MySQL regular expressions are not case-sensitive. ● Escape Character : Use double backslashes ( \\ ) to escape special characters. Key Metacharacters in MySQL Regular Expressions IFNULL VS COALESCE When working with databases, dealing with missing or unknown data is a common challenge. In SQL, this is represented by NULL values. Handling NULL values correctly is crucial for ensuring your data operations are accurate and consistent. Two key functions that help manage NULL values are COALESCE and IFNULL (or ISNULL, depending on the database system). While both functions aim to handle NULL values, they have different features and uses. FEA TURE IFNULL()/ ISNULL() COALESCE Number of Arguments 2 Multiple Standardization Not part of SQL standard (specific to MySQL and SQL Server) Part of SQL standard Versatility Limited for 2 arguments We can handle multiple arguments Return Type Returns the data type of the first argument Returns the data type of the first non-NULL argument Performance Possibly more effective in certain databases for two-ar gument scenarios Effective in managing multiple possibilities NULL values Portability Less portable (database-specific) More portable across different SQL databases Company Related Questions and Platform
